text:
HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY SURGERY — PREOPERATIVE CONSULTATION NOTE

ATTENDING SURGEON: [HPB Surgery, Attending]
SERVICE: Hepatopancreatobiliary and Foregut Surgery
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: [Medical Oncology]
REASON FOR CONSULTATION: Preoperative evaluation and surgical planning for pancreaticoduodenectomy following completion of neoadjuvant therapy for borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with a known diagnosis of pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma who presents for HPB surgical consultation after completing neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (8 cycles) followed by consolidative chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine. The patient and his medical oncologist are seeking surgical evaluation for feasibility of resection given excellent biochemical and radiographic response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old retired pipe fitter with a 30 pack-year smoking history (cessation approximately 15 years prior), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension who initially presented with new-onset obstructive jaundice and epigastric discomfort. Workup at that time revealed a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas on pancreatic protocol CT. This was confirmed to be invasive ductal adenocarcinoma by EUS-guided fine needle aspiration. Molecular profiling demonstrated a KRAS G12V-driven tumor with co-occurring TP53 p.R248W mutation, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A deletion. Microsatellite stable, HRD-negative. At diagnosis, CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL.

The tumor was classified as borderline resectable at initial staging given abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120-150 degrees of circumference and short-segment abutment/involvement of the superior mesenteric vein-portal vein confluence (SMV). Two periportal lymph nodes up to 1.2 cm were radiographically suspicious. Clinical stage at diagnosis: cT3N1M0 (Stage III per AJCC 8th edition). Biliary decompression was performed via ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement given hyperbilirubinemia (total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL at presentation).

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the patient proceeded with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus and infusional). He received 8 cycles over approximately 4 months. The course was complicated by cumulative grade 2-3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (requiring oxaliplatin dose reduction to 80% in cycles 5-8), grade 3 diarrhea after cycle 7 (requiring brief hospitalization, irinotecan dose reduction to 75%), and grade 2 fatigue. Total weight loss from baseline over the chemotherapy period was approximately 7 kg. He achieved a significant partial response: mid-treatment (after cycle 4) CA 19-9 had fallen to 380 U/mL, and at the end of 8 cycles CA 19-9 was 85 U/mL. Imaging after cycle 8 showed the mass had reduced to 2.4 cm, with improved SMA abutment (now less than 90 degrees), improved SMV/PV interface, and normalization of regional lymph nodes (largest now 0.6 cm).

Following multidisciplinary reassessment, the patient underwent consolidative chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine (825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days, later dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID for grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome). He completed the full course without treatment interruption. Toxicities during chemoradiation included grade 2 nausea and vomiting, grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 radiation dermatitis, and grade 1 transaminase elevation that resolved spontaneously.

Pre-surgical restaging CT (approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation) demonstrated further reduction of the pancreatic head mass to 2.1 cm. There is a hypodense central low-attenuation component suggesting treatment-related necrosis. The SMA fat plane appears largely restored with abutment now estimated at less than 60 degrees or possibly no residual contact. The SMV/PV confluence appears patent without definite tumor encasement. Regional lymph nodes are within normal size limits. No distant metastatic disease identified. CA 19-9 at the time of that scan was 62 U/mL — a greater than 95% reduction from baseline.

The patient is referred now for surgical planning for pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). He is anxious but motivated and has been counseled in detail about the risks and expected recovery. He understands the diagnosis and the intent of surgery.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma — borderline resectable, head of pancreas — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin prior to diagnosis; metformin held during chemotherapy days per protocol; currently on basal insulin given altered glucose homeostasis in the setting of pancreatic disease and steroid use with antiemetics)
3. Hypertension — managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily
4. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (at diagnosis, worsened with treatment) — on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (Creon)
5. Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy — grade 1-2, bilateral hands and feet, residual from oxaliplatin
6. Tobacco use history — 30 pack-year, quit 15 years ago

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement (at time of initial diagnosis, for obstructive jaundice) — stent subsequently exchanged; currently in place
- No prior abdominal surgery
- No prior cardiac surgery

FAMILY HISTORY
- No known family history of pancreatic cancer
- Father: coronary artery disease
- Mother: type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension
- No known germline cancer syndromes; germline BRCA1/2 and ATM testing obtained per NCCN recommendation — results: NEGATIVE (no pathogenic germline variants identified)

SOCIAL HISTORY
- Retired pipe fitter; lives with wife
- 30 pack-year smoking history, quit approximately 15 years ago
- Occasional alcohol use (1-2 drinks/week prior to diagnosis; none during treatment)
- No illicit drug use
- Functional at baseline — manages activities of daily living independently; some exercise limitation due to fatigue and neuropathy
- Supportive home environment; wife involved in care

ALLERGIES
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS
1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
2. Insulin glargine (Lantus) 18 units subcutaneously at bedtime (post-treatment diabetes management)
3. Insulin aspart (NovoLog) sliding scale with meals (mealtime coverage)
4. Creon (pancrelipase) 36,000 units (3 capsules of 12,000 units) with each meal, 1-2 capsules with snacks
5. Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (nausea)
6. Duloxetine 30 mg orally daily (initiated for symptomatic management of peripheral neuropathy)
7. Metoprolol succinate 25 mg orally daily (initiated during workup for blood pressure optimization)
8. Multivitamin daily
9. Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Weight loss of approximately 8 kg from pre-diagnosis baseline; stable over the past 4-6 weeks. Fatigue present but improved compared to during chemotherapy (currently grade 1). Appetite improving.
Gastrointestinal: Occasional nausea (grade 1), managed with ondansetron as needed. No vomiting. Loose stools 2-3 times daily (baseline since starting enzyme replacement; improved from prior diarrhea). No frank melena or hematochezia. No abdominal pain at rest; mild epigastric fullness after large meals. No jaundice currently — bilirubin normalized with biliary stenting.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria. Nocturia x 2.
Neurological: Bilateral distal extremity numbness and tingling — feet greater than hands. Mild difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning shirt). Stable for past 4-6 weeks.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning. No joint pain.
Dermatologic: Resolving mild radiation dermatitis over epigastric field. Mild hand-foot changes resolved after capecitabine completion.
Cardiopulmonary: No chest pain, no dyspnea at rest. Mild dyspnea on exertion with climbing stairs (improved from nadir of treatment). No cough.
Endocrine: Blood glucose monitoring at home — readings mostly in the 110-180 mg/dL range fasting; some postprandial spikes to 220-250 mg/dL; discussed with endocrinology.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing male, thin but not cachectic. Alert and oriented x3. In no acute distress. Cooperative and engaged in discussion.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air.
Weight: 72 kg (pre-diagnosis approximately 80 kg)
Height: 5'10" (178 cm)
BMI: approximately 22.7 kg/m2

HEENT: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound. No palpable organomegaly. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds present and active. No signs of ascites (no flank dullness, no shifting dullness). ERCP biliary stent scar visible at epigastrium; no prior surgical incisions.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Mild skin changes on bilateral palms (resolving HFS). Decreased sensation in bilateral feet to monofilament and vibration consistent with sensory neuropathy.
Neurological: Grossly intact cranially. Decreased light touch and vibratory sense bilateral feet > hands. Finger-to-nose intact. Gait mildly cautious but unassisted.
Skin: Mild post-radiation hyperpigmentation in the epigastric treatment field; no open wounds or dermatitis.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY RESULTS

CA 19-9: 62 U/mL (down from 1200 U/mL at diagnosis — >95% reduction)
CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (within normal range)
CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia — normocytic, likely related to nutritional depletion and prior chemotherapy effect), Plt 198 x10^9/L
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR >60; Glucose 142 mg/dL (non-fasting); Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL; Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL; AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L; Total protein 6.4 g/dL, Albumin 3.6 g/dL
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, PTT 28 seconds
HbA1c: 7.8% (most recent, reflects peri-treatment glycemic disruption; improving with insulin regimen)

---

IMAGING REVIEW

Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (pre-surgical restaging, approximately 6 weeks post-chemoradiation completion): Reviewed in detail at today's visit.
- Pancreatic head mass now 2.1 cm, down from 3.8 cm at diagnosis and 2.4 cm after FOLFIRINOX completion. Central low-attenuation/necrotic component present, consistent with treatment effect.
- SMA fat plane appears largely restored. Residual abutment, if any, appears to be less than 60 degrees of circumference — a dramatic improvement from the 120-150 degrees seen at diagnosis. The medial border of the SMA dissection plane appears accessible.
- SMV/PV confluence: appears patent. Short-segment desmoplastic adhesion may be present but no frank encasement identified. The SMV appears reconstructable if short-segment resection is required intraoperatively.
- Regional lymph nodes: normalized in size (largest 0.6 cm). No new or suspicious adenopathy.
- No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No free fluid or ascites.
- Biliary stent in place. Mild intrahepatic biliary dilation proximal to the mass — expected in the setting of ongoing perilesional fibrosis and stent.

I personally reviewed prior imaging side-by-side including the baseline pancreatic protocol CT (3.8 cm mass, SMA abutment 120-150 degrees) and mid-treatment scan (3.1 cm mass, SMA abutment < 90 degrees). The radiographic trajectory has been consistently favorable.

---

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative, germline BRCA/ATM negative) initially classified as borderline resectable (cT3N1M0) who has achieved an excellent and sustained response to neoadjuvant multimodality therapy. CA 19-9 has declined from 1200 to 62 U/mL (>95% reduction). Radiographic tumor regression from 3.8 cm to 2.1 cm with substantial improvement in vascular abutment. In my surgical judgment, this patient has been sufficiently downstaged and is now an appropriate candidate for curative-intent pancreaticoduodenectomy.

Key surgical considerations:
1. SMA involvement: Based on current imaging, residual SMA abutment is minimal (possibly < 60 degrees or absent). I anticipate complete medial SMA dissection is achievable without arterial resection or reconstruction. SMA-first approach will be used to assess true resectability at the outset.
2. SMV/PV involvement: While the confluence appears patent, there is likely desmoplastic adhesion given the initial involvement. We must be prepared for segmental SMV resection and primary reconstruction (end-to-end anastomosis) with primary interposition graft if necessary. Vascular surgery will be on standby.
3. Margins: The significant neoadjuvant response suggests favorable margin status; however, given initial vascular involvement, close margins remain possible, particularly at the SMV groove and posterior surfaces. We will obtain intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin.
4. Performance status: ECOG PS 1. Mild anemia (Hgb 11.4) — will optimize pre-operatively with iron supplementation and nutritional support. Albumin 3.6 — reasonable but would benefit from continued nutritional optimization. Cardiac clearance obtained (pharmacologic stress test negative for ischemia). Pulmonary function adequate for major surgery.
5. Diabetes: Insulin-dependent diabetes management will be coordinated with endocrinology and anesthesia. Blood sugar targets perioperatively established.
6. Neuropathy: Pre-existing grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy noted — anesthesia team aware for positioning and monitoring.
7. Biliary stent: Current plastic stent in place. Plan to remove and replace with metal stent prior to surgery if significant intrahepatic biliary dilation persists or if surgery is delayed further, to reduce the risk of cholangitis. Alternatively, we will proceed directly to resection and remove the stent at the time of surgery given adequate bilirubin and liver function.

---

PLAN

1. Proceed with open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) as planned. Standard reconstruction: duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, antecolic gastrojejunostomy. Pylorus-preserving versus standard Whipple to be determined at time of surgery based on duodenal involvement.

2. Vascular surgery consultation confirmed and placed on standby for potential SMV segmental resection and reconstruction. Pre-op vascular surgery assessment arranged.

3. Pre-operative optimization:
   - Oral nutritional supplementation continued (high-protein, high-calorie formula)
   - Iron supplementation initiated (ferrous sulfate 325 mg BID) to address mild pre-operative anemia; recheck CBC in 3 weeks
   - Perioperative insulin management plan discussed with anesthesia and endocrinology
   - Continue lisinopril up to day prior to surgery; hold on day of surgery
   - Hold metformin (was already discontinued during treatment; not currently on it)
   - Continue Creon with all meals up to and including pre-operative period
   - Pre-operative bowel prep per institutional protocol for pancreaticoduodenectomy

4. Anesthesia pre-assessment completed; cleared for major abdominal surgery under general endotracheal anesthesia with epidural analgesia (thoracic epidural for post-operative pain management).

5. Consent obtained: Patient and spouse met with me today and were counseled extensively regarding risks of pancreaticoduodenectomy including: mortality (< 3% at high-volume center), pancreatic fistula and leak (20-30%), delayed gastric emptying (15-20%), bile leak, wound infection, intraabdominal abscess, need for transfusion, need for vascular reconstruction, conversion to exploratory laparotomy only if widespread peritoneal disease found at exploration (unlikely given no imaging evidence), venous thromboembolism, and longer-term issues including new or worsening pancreatic exocrine and endocrine insufficiency. Patient verbalized understanding and signed informed consent documents including consent for potential SMV/PV resection and reconstruction.

6. Patient instructed to continue current medication regimen as above until day of surgery (with exceptions noted).

7. Surgery scheduled. Patient to present for pre-operative nursing assessment and labs the morning of the procedure. NPO after midnight the night before.

8. Anticipated hospital stay: 7-10 days for uncomplicated course; longer if vascular reconstruction required or if post-operative complications arise.

9. Post-operative plan: Standard Whipple recovery pathway. Post-op drain amylase checked day 3. CA 19-9 post-operatively at 4 weeks. Oncology follow-up arranged for adjuvant therapy planning (target initiation at 6-8 weeks post-operatively pending recovery).

Patient expressed understanding and is agreeable to proceed. He appears motivated, well-supported by family, and has realistic expectations about recovery. He understands that while imaging and biochemistry are encouraging, the final pathological assessment — including margin status and lymph node involvement — will help guide further adjuvant decision-making.

Total face time with patient and family: approximately 60 minutes.

— [Attending, HPB Surgery]
[Electronically signed]
